content.text:
Artificial Intelligence is everywhere, generating constant buzz and media attention. While it's tempting to dive right into the latest models, a true understanding of AI requires a deeper look into its past.

To grasp what makes today's AI tick, we must start at the beginning. This series will attempt to bridge the gap from the invention of the first transistor in 1947 to the powerful and often controversial AI of today. We'll explore the foundational knowledge that is essential before we can truly understand what AI is and what it is not.

It's a long road, but a necessary one. Join me as we journey through an abridged overview of computer science and discover the foundations of the modern world of computation.

The history of computer science, with its theoretical giants like Alan Turing and Claude Shannon, began long before the first physical computer was built. While their work on logic and information theory is foundational, our story will start with the practical invention: the transistor.

Honestly, on its own, it's really not that special. A transistor is essentially a switch. It does the same job as a light switch on your wall, but instead of being flipped by a human hand, it's controlled by a tiny electrical signal.

One switch on its own isn't very powerful. But when we start combining them, that's where the magic happens, and it's from here we will take the first step on our journey together.

Yesterday, I introduced the transistor and its standard pictogram but unlike the switch, there's no obvious way to see if it's on or off (closed or open). From here, we will start building simple circuits by slowly introducing new components.

To understand this, let's first look at a basic circuit. In this simple circuit, we see a power source (battery), a closed switch, and a lightbulb. Because the circuit is "closed" the current can flow and light up the bulb. If the circuit was "open", the current would not be able to flow and the lightbulb would be off. The order of these three components in this circuit does not matter; the behavior would be the same.

Now we can see how to control the behavior of the transistor and, therefore, turn the light on and off. In the accompanying diagram, a physical switch controls a small current that flows to the transistor. When we close this physical switch, it allows current to activate the electrical switch (the transistor), which in turn closes the main circuit and turns on the lightbulb. This example is a little contrived because having both a switch and a transistor is redundant, but that won't be the case going forward.

If we use two transitors like the ones configured in this circuit, we can create interesting behavior. We are interested in the bottom lightbulb, which we will call the output. The top two lightbulb and switches will be the inputs. As you can see in the animation, it's only when the first switch and the second switch are closed that the output lightbulb will turn on. This is an example of an AND gate. The name is pretty self-explanatory: the output is only on when both inputs are on. All the other combinations of inputs will result in the output light being off.
content.caption: This is an AND gate. The output is on only when both inputs are on.

content.text: Let me do a little housekeeping and organize our inputs on the left and the output on the right. I've also configured the input lights after the switch to show if the input is on or off. Now i've organized the two transistors in such a way that if input 1 or input 2 is on, the output will be on. Low and behold, we have an OR gate! This is another fundamental logic gate that is used in many circuits. The output is on if either input 1 or input 2 is on, or both are on.
content.caption: This is an OR gate. The output is on if either input 1 or input 2 is on, or both are on.
